sections.title: SQL Basics & Syntax
sections.content:
SQL (Structured Query Language) is a standardized language for managing relational databases. It follows a client-server model where queries are sent to a database server which processes and returns results.

Key Concepts:
• Case Insensitivity: SQL keywords are case-insensitive (SELECT = select), but values are case-sensitive
• Statements: SQL code consists of statements (commands) that end with a semicolon (;)
• Comments: Single-line (--) or multi-line (/* */)
• Data Types: INT, FLOAT, VARCHAR(size), TEXT, DATE, TIMESTAMP, BOOLEAN
• NULL: Represents missing or unknown values (different from empty strings or 0)
• Operators: Arithmetic (+, -, *, /), Comparison (=, !=, <>, <, >, <=, >=), Logical (AND, OR, NOT)

Basic Structure:
SELECT column1, column2 FROM table_name WHERE condition ORDER BY column1;

Execution Order: WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → ORDER BY

Database Objects:
• Database: Container for tables and other objects
• Table: Structured data stored in rows and columns
• Column: Vertical set of data values
• Row: Single record in a table
• Primary Key: Unique identifier for each row
• Foreign Key: Reference to primary key in another table

sections.title: DDL — Data Definition Language
DML — Data Manipulation Language
SELECT & Filtering
sections.content:
SELECT is the most commonly used SQL command to retrieve data.

Basic SELECT:
Syntax: SELECT column1, column2 FROM table_name;
• SELECT *: Returns all columns
• SELECT DISTINCT: Returns unique values only
• SELECT col AS alias: Renames column in result

WHERE Clause (Filtering Rows):
Syntax: SELECT * FROM table_name WHERE condition;
Conditions: =, !=, <>, <, >, <=, >=
• BETWEEN: WHERE age BETWEEN 20 AND 30;
• IN: WHERE city IN ('NYC', 'LA', 'Chicago');
• LIKE: WHERE name LIKE 'J%'; (% = multiple chars, _ = single char)
• IS NULL / IS NOT NULL: Check for null values

ORDER BY (Sorting):
Syntax: SELECT * FROM table_name ORDER BY column1 ASC, column2 DESC;
• ASC: Ascending (default), DESC: Descending
• Multiple columns: Sorts by first, then second, etc.

LIMIT & OFFSET:
Syntax: SELECT * FROM table_name LIMIT 10 OFFSET 5;
• LIMIT 10: Returns first 10 rows
• OFFSET 5: Skips first 5 rows
• Useful for pagination

Logical Operators in WHERE:
• AND: Both conditions true
• OR: At least one condition true
• NOT: Negates condition
Example: SELECT * FROM products WHERE price > 100 AND category = 'Electronics';

CASE Statement:
SELECT name, CASE WHEN age >= 18 THEN 'Adult' ELSE 'Minor' END AS status FROM users;
Multiple conditions: WHEN condition1 THEN result1 WHEN condition2 THEN result2 ELSE default

sections.title: Joins
Aggregate Functions & GROUP BY
sections.content:
Aggregate functions perform calculations on a set of values and return a single result.

Common Aggregate Functions:
• COUNT(): Count number of rows
  Example: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM employees;
• SUM(): Add values
  Example: SELECT SUM(salary) FROM employees;
• AVG(): Calculate average
  Example: SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees;
• MIN(): Return minimum value
  Example: SELECT MIN(salary) FROM employees;
• MAX(): Return maximum value
  Example: SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees;
• COUNT(DISTINCT col): Count unique values
  Example: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT city) FROM customers;

GROUP BY:
Syntax: SELECT department, COUNT(*) FROM employees GROUP BY department;
Groups rows by one or more columns. Aggregate functions then operate on each group.
All non-aggregated columns in SELECT must be in GROUP BY.

HAVING Clause:
Syntax: SELECT department, AVG(salary) FROM employees GROUP BY department HAVING AVG(salary) > 50000;
Filters groups (like WHERE for GROUP BY). Applied after grouping.
WHERE filters individual rows; HAVING filters groups.

Execution Order:
FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → aggregates → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY

Complete Example:
SELECT dept_id, COUNT(*) as emp_count, AVG(salary) as avg_salary
FROM employees
WHERE hire_date > '2020-01-01'
GROUP BY dept_id
HAVING COUNT(*) > 5
ORDER BY avg_salary DESC;

sections.title: Subqueries & Views
Indexes & Normalization
sections.content:
Indexes improve query performance. Normalization ensures data integrity and reduces redundancy.

INDEXES:
Data structure that speeds up data retrieval. Without index: full table scan. With index: optimized search (binary search).

Types of Indexes:
1. Primary Key Index: Automatically created, unique, non-null
2. Unique Index: CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_email ON users(email);
3. Composite Index: CREATE INDEX idx_dept_salary ON employees(dept_id, salary);
4. Full-Text Index: CREATE FULLTEXT INDEX idx_description ON products(description);
5. Partial Index: CREATE INDEX idx_active ON users(id) WHERE status = 'active';

Index Tradeoffs:
Advantages: Faster SELECT, WHERE, ORDER BY, JOIN operations
Disadvantages: Slower INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE, uses disk space, requires maintenance

When to Index:
• Columns frequently used in WHERE, JOIN, ORDER BY
• Large tables with selective searches
• Foreign keys
• NOT on small tables or low-cardinality columns

Viewing/Dropping Indexes:
SHOW INDEXES FROM table_name;
DROP INDEX idx_name ON table_name;

NORMALIZATION:
Goal: Eliminate data redundancy and improve data integrity.
Benefits: Saves space, maintains consistency, prevents anomalies
Drawback: More joins needed (performance trade-off)

Normal Forms:

1st Normal Form (1NF):
Each column contains atomic (single) values. No repeating groups.
Bad: hobbies = 'reading, coding' | Good: One hobby per row

2nd Normal Form (2NF):
Meets 1NF. All non-key columns depend on entire primary key (not partial).
Bad: order_id, product_id, customer_name | Good: Separate tables

3rd Normal Form (3NF):
Meets 2NF. No transitive dependencies (non-key columns depend only on primary key).
Bad: student_id, major_id, major_name | Good: Separate tables

Boyce-Codd Normal Form (BCNF):
Stricter version of 3NF. Every determinant is a candidate key.

Denormalization:
Intentionally violating normalization for performance. Trade-off: faster queries, duplicate data.
Used in data warehouses and analytics. Normalize first, denormalize based on needs.
